content.claim: Cross-national econometric evidence using TIMSS/PIRLS/PISA (Hanushek & Woessmann) finds that early tracking increases educational inequality and shows a tendency to reduce mean performance, with Hungary named as an early-tracking country.
content.context: Fresh live research through the International comparison lens
limitations: This finding is explicitly contested: a large replication study using 20 years of PISA/PIRLS/TIMSS data across 75 countries concluded that 'research to date on the effects of between-school tracking on inequalities has been inconclusive'.
International comparative studies rely on small country-level samples (naturally limited number of countries), reducing precision of effect estimates.
The study does not isolate Hungary's specific institutional context, including the interaction between tracking and unrestricted free school choice.
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
